content.full_text:
Fnrn PolrrrcAr- PnACrrces C oMMrssroN
"ll8 J Street o Suite 620 o Sacramenro. CA 9-581,1-2.329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
June 30,2009
Phil Jay
District Counsel
San Joaquin Valley Air Pollution District
1990 E. Gettysburg Avenue
Fresno, CA93726-0244
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-09-142
Dear Mr. Jay:
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of San Joaquin Valley
Unified Air Pollution Control District ("District") Goveming Board member Leroy
Ornellas regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").1 Please note this letter is based orrthe facts presented. The Fair Political
Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders
assistance. (ln re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.)
In addition, the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct.
(Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to
evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained
in this letter apply only to prospective actions.
Because yourseek general information, we are treating your request as one for
informal assistance.-
I The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections I 8 I l0 through I 8997 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
2 Infbrmal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831 l4; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed).


File No. A-09-142
Page No. 2
QrrEsTroN
May Board member Ornellas participate in decisions to amend rules regulating
pollution emissions from "large confined animal facilities" that may impact his dairy
farm?
CONCLUSION
Board member Ornellas may not participate in District decisions involving pollution
emission controls on large confined animal facilities if the decisions will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his dairy farm.
FACTS
You are the civil legal advisor for the District. You seek advice on behalf of
Board member Leroy Ornellas regarding his duties under the Act.
The District's jurisdiction covers the eight counties of the San Joaquin Valley.
Under the federal and state Clean Air Acts, the District regulates stationary sources of air
pollution through the permitting programs and prohibitory rules. The District Goveming
Board adopts various rules and regulations requiring pollution controls on thousands of
businesses in the Valley. During our telephone conversation of June 9, 2009, you stated
that compliance with these rules and regulations is often costly for businesses.
Senate Bill 700, enacted in 2004, requires the District to regulate agricultural
operations. Under Health and Safety Code Section 40724.6, the District is required to
adopt regulations to control pollution from "large confined animal facilities" ("LCAF")
as defined by the California Air Resources Board ("CARB"). These regulations apply to
large dairies, poultry operations and hog farms. For purposes of the regulation, CARB
defines a large confined animal facility as having over 1,000 milking cows.
In accordance with the Health and Safety Code, the District adopted Rule 4570 in
2006. District Rule 4570 mandates covered facilities (large confined animal facilities, as
defined by the State of California) implement various control measures designed to
reduce emissions from their facilities. After various court challenges, the Fresno
Superior Court ordered the rule be set aside until a health assessment is performed. The
District set the rule aside in May of 2009.
Boardmember Omellas owns and operates a dairy that is below the 1,000 milking
cow requirement. There will be future public hearings before the District's Governing
Board relating to regulations on confined animal facilities.
The Governing Board will consider a health study and other relevant information
to decide whether to readopt the Rule without any changes or direct the staff to bring


File No. A-09-142
Page No. 3
back a revised version for future consideration by the Board. Supervisor Ornellas' dairy
is not a LCAF as defined by the State of California. The main purpose for this hearing is
to adopt a rule that meets the Health & Safety Code requirements for large confined
animal facilities. Although the Board cannot change the size definition for what
constitutes a LCAF, it is likely that there will be suggestions from the public to have the
Rule apply to smaller dairies. This would go beyond the applicable requirements under
the state law, but it would be within the Board's discretion to do so. Applying varying
degrees of controls to large dairies (larger than that owned by Superviroio*"llus) wiI
also be subject of this hearing.
A future hearing will also consider possible enhancements to the rule that may
entail lowering the triggering number of animals from the current 1,000 milking cow
limit down to 500. Under this scenario, Supervisor Ornellas' dairy may become subject
to the amended rule.
You wish to know whether Ornellas should disqualify himself from decisions
considering future amendments that may reduce the threshold to a point where his facility
is covered.
A1\[ALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participaring in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in
which the official has a financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's
economic interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has
adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an individual has a
disqualifying conflict of interest in a given govemmental decision.
Step One: Is Board member Ornellas a,,public official?r'
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to "public officials."
(Sections 87100, 87103; Regulation 18700(bXl).) A "public official" is ..every member,
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency . . .."
(Section 82048.) As a member of the District Governing Board, Board member Omellas
is a public official within the meaning of the Act.
Step Two: Will Board member Ornellas be making, participating in making, or
infl uencin g governmental decisions?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter. obligates or


File No. A-09-142
Page No.4
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.L.)
A public official "participates in making a govemmental decision" when, acting
within the authority of his or her position and without significant intervening substantive
review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker
regarding the govemmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.)
A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or appears before any
member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)
Board member Ornellas will be making, participating in making, or influencing
governmental decisions when he makes, participates in making, or influences decisions to
amend Board regulations implementing measures to control and reduce pollution
emissions from LCAFs.
Step Three: What are Board member Ornellas'economic interests?
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on any of the official's economic
interests, described as follows:
. An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or
indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a));
or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703.1(b).)
o An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation 18703.2.)
o An economic interest in a source of income, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87 103(c); Regulation I 8703.3.)
o An economic interest in a source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to
$420 or more within 12 months prior ro the decision. (Section 87r03(e);
Regulation 18703.4.)
o An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule.
(Section 87 103; Regulation 1 8703.5.)


File No. A-09-142
Page No. 5
Based on the facts you provided, Board member Ornellas has an economic
interest in his diary farm, as a business entity and a source of income. He also has an
economic interest in the business by virtue of his position if he is a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee or holds any position of management. For purposes of this
analysis, we assume that Board member Ornellas has an investment of $2,000 or more in
the dairy business.
Personal Financial Effects:
Under Regulation 18703.5 Board member Ornellas, like all public officials, has a
potentially disqualifying economic interest in his personal finances. In particular, a
government decision has a personal financial effect on a public official if the decision
will result in the personal expenses, income, assets, or liabilities of the official increasing
or decreasing.' (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)
Because you have not provided any information regarding other potential
economic interests, including Board member Ornellas' personal finances, we limit our
analysis only to the interests identified above.
Step Four: wilt Board member ornellas'economic interests be direcfly or
indirectly involved in the decisions?
Business Entities and Sources of Income
A person, including business entities and sources of income, is directly involved
in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either directly or indirectly by
an agent:
"(l) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
conceming the decision before the official or the official's agency. A
person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the issuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person." (Regulation
18704.1(a).)
Because Board member Omellas' dairy farm does not appear to be initiating
proceedings nor does it appear to be a named party or subject of a proceeding, it is not
directly involved in the project decisions.
I However, neither the financial effects on the value of their real property or their business entity
shall be considered when determining whether a governmental decision has a material t'inancial efTect on
their personal finances, including those of their immediate family. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)


File No. A-09-142
Page No. 6
When an economic interest is not directly involved in a governmental decision, it
is indirectly involved. (Regulation 18704.)
Step Five: What is the applicable materiality standard?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a govemmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material.
(Regulation 18700(a).) Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect on an economic interest will be material, depending on the
nature of the economic interest and whether that interest is directly or indirectly involved
in the agency's decision.
Indirectly Involved Business Entities:
The materiality standards for indirectly involved sources of income that are
business entities are set forth in Regulation 18705.1(c). The appropriate standard depends
on the relative size of the entity. Because you did not mention the size of Board member
Ornellas' dairy farm, we cannot analyze which standard to use to determine whether the
financial effects of a govemmental decision will be considered material. However,
assuming that the dairy business is relatively small and not publicly traded, Regulation
18705.1(c)(4) applies. We enclose a copy of Regulation 18705.1 so you can determine
which materiality standard would apply.
It provides that a financial effect is considered material if:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
$20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The govemmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating
existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more.,,
If Board member Ornellas' dairy farm is a small business that is not publicly
traded, and the reasonably foreseeable financial effect on his business meets any of the
above thresholds, the financial effect of a decision is deemed material.


File No. A-09-142
Page No. 7
Step Six: Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial.effect of the governmental
decisions on Board member Ornellas'economic interests will meet the applicable
materiality standard?
An effect meets the "reasonably foreseeable" threshold if the effect is
"substantially likely." (Regulation 18706; In re Thorner (1975) I FppC ops. 19g.) A
financial effect need not be a certainty to be considered reasonably foreseeable. On the
other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
Based on the information you provided, it appears that there may be decisions
before the board that could materially affect Board member Ornellas' dairy farm,
especially if the decisions involve adjustment of, or even whether or not to adjust,
pollution control rules to include smaller farms similar to the board member's. You had
stated that compliance with these rules could be "costly" for businesses.
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is reasonably foreseeable at the
time a decision is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (ln re
Thorner, supra, at 198.) Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in
providing advice (In re Oglesby, supra, atTl), the foreseeability of a particular financial
effect is a determination that must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of
the public official.
Steps Seven and Eight: Public Generally and Legally Required Participation
Exceptions.
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is
reasonably foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the
govemmental decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from
its effect on the public generally (Section 87103, Regulations 18700(b)(7) and 18707(a)),
or if the official is legally required to participate (Section 87103; Regulation 18708). you
have not presented any facts indicating that either of these exceptions is applicable to
Board member Ornellas' situation.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Counsel
By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Legal Division
ERjgl

sections.conclusion:
Board member Ornellas may not participate in District decisions involving pollution
emission controls on large confined animal facilities if the decisions will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his dairy farm.

embedding.qa_text:
CONCLUSION: Board member Ornellas may not participate in District decisions involving pollution
emission controls on large confined animal facilities if the decisions will have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his dairy farm.
